title: Lung Cancer — NSCLC
oneLiner: A heterogeneous group of epithelial lung malignancies causing local invasion, distant metastases, and paraneoplastic syndromes; leading cause of cancer death worldwide.

sections.Lung Cancer — Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: **One-liner:** A heterogeneous group of epithelial lung malignancies causing local invasion, distant metastases, and paraneoplastic syndromes; leading cause of cancer death worldwide.
sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 67-year-old man, a heavy smoker with hypertension, presents to the respiratory clinic with a 4-week history of progressive dyspnoea on mild exertion and a persistent, dry cough. He also reports vague right shoulder pain and intermittent hoarseness over the last 2 weeks. His wife notes he has lost about 6 kg over the past 2 months without trying. On examination, he has finger clubbing, a dull percussion note over the right upper zone, and reduced air entry. His voice is slightly hoarse. Chest X-ray shows a 4 cm right hilar mass with irregular margins and some volume loss on the right.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Heavy smoker, >60 years** → Age and smoking are the strongest risk factors; squamous cell and small cell are most strongly linked, but adenocarcinoma also occurs in smokers and never-smokers.
- **Progressive dyspnoea + persistent cough** → Airway obstruction or compression by tumour; may also indicate pleural effusion or lymphangitic carcinomatosis.
- **Shoulder pain** → Pancoast tumour (superior sulcus tumour) invading brachial plexus; also consider bone metastasis.
- **Hoarseness** → Recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy (left more commonly affected due to longer course around aortic arch) → tumour or mediastinal lymph node compression.
- **Weight loss, finger clubbing** → Systemic effects (cachexia, paraneoplastic syndromes; clubbing suggests chronic pulmonary disease or specific lung cancer types, especially adenocarcinoma and squamous cell).
- **Dull percussion, reduced air entry** → Pleural effusion (more common in adenocarcinoma) or lung consolidation/atelectasis from central airway obstruction.
- **CXR: hilar mass, irregular margins, volume loss** → Central tumour with post-obstructive atelectasis or collapse; volume loss suggests obstruction.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Presenting as a solitary pulmonary nodule (SPN) on imaging** – asymptomatic, found incidentally. Could be early-stage NSCLC or benign. Pattern: no smoking history, no symptoms, nodule slowly growing. Don’t assume small = benign; need CT surveillance or PET.
2. **Paraneoplastic syndrome as first clue** – e.g. hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy (clubbing + periostitis) may present before respiratory symptoms; also hypercalcaemia from PTHrP (squamous cell) → confusion, polyuria, abdominal pain before cough.
3. **Metastatic disease first** – e.g. a patient with new headache and a solitary brain lesion on CT; biopsy may show adenocarcinoma lung primary. Or pathological fracture from bone metastasis. No chest symptoms at onset.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
NSCLC accounts for ~85% of all lung cancers. It is a malignancy arising from epithelial cells of the respiratory tract, with three main histological subtypes: **adenocarcinoma** (most common, often peripheral), **squamous cell carcinoma** (central, cavitating, strong smoking link), and **large cell carcinoma** (less common, aggressive). Other rarer subtypes exist.

Clinically, this matters because:
- **High prevalence** – lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer death globally. As an FY1 you will see it on respiratory wards, oncological clinics, and acute medical take (for complications like massive haemoptysis, SVCO, hypercalcaemia).
- **Late presentation** – 70% are already advanced (stage III or IV) at diagnosis. Early detection improves survival, but many patients present with symptoms that mimic benign disease (e.g. pneumonia, bronchitis) or with silent metastatic deposits.
- **Common pitfalls** – missing a lung mass on CXR (especially behind the heart or in the mediastinum); dismissing haemoptysis as bronchitis; not ordering appropriate staging (PET-CT, EBUS) before surgery; ignoring paraneoplastic syndromes.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:**
NSCLC originates from a series of genetic mutations (e.g. EGFR, KRAS, ALK rearrangements, p53 loss) that drive uncontrolled epithelial cell proliferation. These mutations are often induced by carcinogens in tobacco smoke. The tumour grows locally, invading bronchi, lung parenchyma, pleura, and chest wall. It then metastasises via lymphatics (hilar, mediastinal, supraclavicular) and bloodstream (bone, brain, liver, adrenal glands).

**Mechanism → sign:**
- **Airway obstruction** → persistent cough, wheeze, post-obstructive pneumonia, collapse (volume loss on CXR). Central squamous cell carcinomas are most likely to cause this.
- **Pleural invasion / effusion** → dyspnoea, dull percussion, reduced breath sounds. Adenocarcinoma commonly spreads to pleura and produces bloody effusion.
- **Chest wall / brachial plexus invasion** → Pancoast tumour (apical) causes shoulder pain, Horner’s syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis), arm weakness/paraesthesia. This is a surgical emergency if resectable.
- **Recurrent laryngeal nerve compression** → hoarseness. Left nerve is more vulnerable because it loops around the aortic arch and is often compressed by enlarged subaortic (aortopulmonary window) nodes.
- **Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO)** → swelling of face/neck, dilated veins, headache. Usually from right-sided tumours compressing SVC.
- **Paraneoplastic syndromes** – tumour secreting hormone-like substances:
  - **PTHrP** (squamous cell) → hypercalcaemia → confusion, polyuria, constipation, short QT on ECG.
  - **SIADH** (more common in SCLC, but can occur in NSCLC) → hyponatraemia → confusion, seizures.
  - **Clubbing / hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy** (adeno, squamous) → periostitis of long bones, painful joints; mechanism unclear – may involve growth factors or vascular shunting.
- **Cachexia** → weight loss, muscle wasting, anaemia; pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) from tumour-host interaction.
- **Metastatic spread** – brain: headache, seizures, focal neurology; bone: bone pain, pathological fracture; liver: RUQ pain, elevated LFTs; adrenal: usually asymptomatic.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
The vignette’s shoulder pain + hoarseness + clubbing suggests a superior sulcus tumour with recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement and paraneoplastic osteoarthropathy – all tying to local invasion and systemic secretion.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem clue pointing to it |
|----------|-------|---------------------------|
| **Most common** | Tobacco smoking (active or passive) | “Heavy smoker, 40 pack-years” |
| Occupational | Asbestos fibres (synergistic with smoking) | “Works in construction/shipyard” – also risk of mesothelioma |
| Occupational | Radon exposure (mining, home radon) | “Lives in area with high radon” |
| Occupational | Other carcinogens: arsenic, chromium, nickel, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons | “History of work in smelting, coal gasification” |
| Environmental | Air pollution (PM2.5) | “Lives in urban area with high pollution” |
| Genetic | Family history of lung cancer in first-degree relative | “Multiple family members with lung cancer, never smoked” |
| Genetic | Pre-existing lung disease (COPD, pulmonary fibrosis) | “COPD, IPF – both increase risk independent of smoking” |
| Idiopathic | Adenocarcinoma in never-smokers | “Never smoker, female, East Asian – often EGFR mutation” |
| Infective | No direct infective cause; but chronic inflammation may promote carcinogenesis | e.g. history of tuberculosis scar → scar carcinoma (rare) |
| Iatrogenic | Prior thoracic radiotherapy | “Previous Hodgkin’s lymphoma treated with mantle field radiation” |

**Highlight:** The most common cause is **tobacco smoking** (c.90% of lung cancer deaths in men, 80% in women). The risk is dose-dependent (pack-years) and persists years after quitting.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**
Diagnosis requires **histological or cytological confirmation** from a biopsy (bronchoscopic, CT-guided, EBUS, or pleural fluid). Staging uses the **TNM 8th edition** (T: tumour size/invasion, N: lymph node involvement, M: metastases). PET-CT and mediastinal staging are critical for deciding surgery.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Chest X-ray** | Solitary pulmonary nodule, hilar mass, pleural effusion, collapse, cavitation | First-line imaging for symptoms (cough, haemoptysis, dyspnoea) | Solid nodule >3 cm with spiculated margins is highly suspicious. Look for loss of lung volume, bony destruction. Sensitivity ~77% for central tumours, lower for peripheral. |
| **CT chest + upper abdomen** (with contrast) | Detailed anatomy: size, location, invasion of mediastinum/chest wall, lymphadenopathy, liver/adrenal metastases | Staging and surgical planning; best for local invasion | Solid nodule with spiculation, pleural tags. Hilar/mediastinal nodes >1 cm short axis suspicious. |
| **PET-CT** (FDG-PET) | Metabolic activity of tumour and nodes | Staging to detect distant metastases; helps differentiate benign vs malignant nodules | Standardised uptake value (SUVmax) >2.5 suggests malignancy. But false positives: infection, inflammation (sarcoid, TB). |
| **Bronchoscopy + biopsy** | Endobronchial lesion, can get tissue, brushings, washings | Diagnose central tumours; also for staging (EBUS-TBNA of mediastinal nodes) | Adenocarcinoma: glandular pattern, TTF-1 positive. Squamous: keratin pearls, p40 positive. |
| **CT-guided biopsy** | Peripheral lesion sample | When bronchoscopy cannot reach (e.g. subpleural nodule) | Risk of pneumothorax ~15-20%. Ensure cytology from multiple passes. |
| **EBUS-TBNA** | Mediastinal/hilar lymph node sampling | Minimally invasive mediastinal staging (N2/N3) | Positive for malignancy = advanced stage. If negative, may still need mediastinoscopy. |
| **Pleural fluid cytology** | Malignant cells in pleural effusion | Rapid diagnosis if pleural effusion present | Sensitivity ~60-80% with multiple taps. If negative but high suspicion, consider thoracoscopy. |
| **Bloods** | FBC, U&E, LFTs, calcium, LDH | Baseline, detect metastases/paraneoplastic | Hypercalcaemia (squamous), hyponatraemia (SIADH), elevated ALP/bone mets |
| **Sputum cytology** | Malignant cells | Non-invasive – but low sensitivity (<50%) | Only used if unable to undergo other procedures (rarely) |

**Pattern recognition:**
- **Single finding that confirms diagnosis:** Histology showing malignant cells consistent with primary lung carcinoma (non-small cell type by immunohistochemistry).
- **Single finding that rules out:** Total absence of tumour after complete resection and no evidence of disease on surveillance (never completely rule out – can recur).
- **Key staging pitfall:** Do not proceed to surgery without PET-CT and mediastinal node sampling if nodes are enlarged on CT – unsuspected N2 disease changes management to chemoradiotherapy.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
Management depends on stage, histology, performance status, and molecular markers. For this entry we focus on NSCLC overall.

**Immediate (first hour) – if acute presentation with complication:**
- **Massive haemoptysis** (rare but life-threatening): call anaesthetics/critical care, position patient with affected side down, secure airway, consider rigid bronchoscopy if available. Do not delay.
- **SVCO with airway compromise:** head-up position, oxygen, IV dexamethasone 8 mg stat, then 4 mg qds. Urgent CT + referral to radiotherapy or oncology for stent.
- **Hypercalcaemia (symptomatic):** IV fluids (0.9% saline 1 L stat, then 2-3 L/24h), IV bisphosphonate e.g. zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over 15 min (renal monitoring). Correct calcium slowly.

**Ward (24-72 hours) – diagnostic and early treatment planning:**
- **Complete staging** as per investigations above.
- **Multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion** – at which stage and treatment plan are decided.
- **Early stage I-II** (resectable): surgery (lobectomy preferred; sublobar if poor lung function) + mediastinal lymph node dissection. If medically inoperable → stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR).
- **Stage III** (locally advanced): combination of chemoradiotherapy (platinum doublet + radiotherapy). Consider durvalumab (checkpoint inhibitor) after chemoradiotherapy for unresectable stage III.
- **Stage IV** (metastatic): palliative intent. if driver mutation (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF V600E) → targeted therapy (e.g. osimertinib 80 mg PO daily for EGFR; alectinib 600 mg PO bd for ALK). If no mutation → immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) +/- chemotherapy (platinum doublet).
- **Paraneoplastic syndrome management:** treat underlying tumour; also specific measures (e.g. hypercalcaemia fluids + bisphosphonates; hyponatraemia fluid restriction ± tolvaptan, but avoid rapid correction).

**Long-term:**
- Surveillance: CT chest every 6-12 months for 2 years, then annually for up to 5 years. No routine surveillance for stage IV unless on trial.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do not give steroids for uncomplicated cough or dyspnoea unless proven COPD exacerbation – can mask progression.
  - Do not perform prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) in NSCLC – only indicated in SCLC.
  - Do not assume a new pulmonary nodule is benign because it’s small – always re-image.
  - Do not start chemotherapy without confirming histology and molecular testing (NGS panel). You can cause harm without target.

**Common pitfalls:**
- Missing a deep vein thrombosis/pulmonary embolism in a patient with lung cancer (hypercoagulable). Consider LMWH.
- Underdosing opioids for cancer pain due to fear of dependence – use WHO ladder, morphine is safe.
- Not discussing smoking cessation at every opportunity – it improves outcomes and reduces complications.

**Drug doses inline:**
- **Dexamethasone for SVCO:** 8 mg IV stat, then 4 mg PO/IV QDS for 3-5 days then taper.
- **Zoledronic acid for hypercalcaemia:** 4 mg IV (over ≥15 min) as single dose; must be rehydrated first; monitor renal function.
- **Osimertinib (EGFR T790M/prevalent):** 80 mg PO once daily, with or without food; adjust for side effects (diarrhoea, rash, pneumonitis).
- **Alectinib (ALK-positive):** 600 mg PO twice daily.
- **Pembrolizumab (PD-1 inhibitor):** 200 mg IV every 3 weeks or 400 mg every 6 weeks for up to 2 years (first line in PD-L1 ≥50% and no driver mutation).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | NSCLC | Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC) | Lung Abscess | Tuberculosis (pulmonary) |
|---------|-------|-------------------------------|--------------|--------------------------|
| **Pain** | Focal, often dull, may be referred (shoulder) | Usually none early; later chest pain from mets | Pleuritic, localized, with fever | Pleuritic or dull, may be chronic |
| **Exam** | Clubbing, lymphadenopathy (supraclavicular), focal signs (effusion, collapse) | Lymphadenopathy (often bulky), SVCO, no clubbing | Fever, signs of consolidation, halitosis | Crackles, weight loss, night sweats, lymphadenopathy |
| **ECG** | Normal unless SVCO → sinus tachycardia, or hypercalcaemia → short QT | May have hypokalaemia/hyponatraemia-related changes | Normal | Normal |
| **Labs** | Ca++↑ (squamous), Na+↓ (SIADH in both but more SCLC), LDH↑ | LDH often ↑↑, Na+↓ (SIADH), Ca++ rarely high | WBC ↑, CRP ↑, culture positive | T-SPOT/IGRA positive, sputum AFB positive, LDH may be normal |
| **Imaging** | Hilar mass, spiculated nodule, cavitation (squamous), pleural effusion | Large mediastinal mass, no clubbing, rapid growth | Cavity with air-fluid level, thick wall, surrounding consolidation | Apical infiltrates, cavitation (cavity thin-walled vs thick in abscess), hilar lymphadenopathy, Ghon complex |
| **Key differentiating feature** | Histology; smoking history; peripheral or central; molecular mutations | Very rapid growth; early brain mets; paraneoplastic syndromes (SIADH, LEMS) more common | Fever, purulent sputum, rapid resolution with antibiotics | Exposure history; positive AFB; response to anti-TB therapy; often younger patients |

**Note:** Granulomatous disease (sarcoid, silicosis) can also mimic bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Sarcoid usually has no clubbing, often has erythema nodosum, and ACE levels elevated.
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complications:**

| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO)** | Facial/neck swelling, dyspnoea, headache, dilated collateral veins on chest | Pemberton’s sign (arm raise → venous engorgement), stridor if tracheal compression | Urgent CT + dexamethasone + radiotherapy/stenting. Assess airway patency. |
| **Massive haemoptysis** (>200 mL in 24h) | Coughing blood, hypotension, respiratory distress | Airway compromise, haemodynamic instability | Crash call; intubate if GCS <8 or hypoxia; rigid bronchoscopy for tamponade; embolisation if fails. |
| **Malignant pleural effusion** | Dyspnoea, dull percussion, reduced breath sounds | Chest X-ray shows blunting of costophrenic angle, CT shows fluid | Therapeutic thoracentesis for relief; pleurodesis (talc) or indwelling pleural catheter for recurrent effusion. |
| **Brain metastases** | Headache, seizures, focal weakness, confusion | CT/MRI brain shows enhancing lesions (often solitary) | Dexamethasone 4 mg QDS, whole brain RT or stereotactic radiosurgery; consider control of primary. |
| **Spinal cord compression** | Back pain, leg weakness, sensory level, bladder/bowel dysfunction | MRI spine shows extradural mass | High-dose dexamethasone (16 mg IV stat, then 4 mg QDS); urgent radiotherapy or surgery. |
| **Hypercalcaemia (paraneoplastic)** | Confusion, polyuria, constipation, nausea | ECG: short QT; Ca >2.8 mmol/L | IV fluids, bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid), treat tumour. |
| **Post-obstructive pneumonia** | Productive cough, fever, dyspnoea, signs of consolidation | CXR shows infiltrate distal to mass | Antibiotics (e.g. co-amoxiclav); if recurrent, consider bronchoscopic debulking or radiotherapy. |
| **Thromboembolism (VTE)** | Swollen leg (DVT), sudden dyspnoea/pleuritic pain (PE) | Wells score high; D-dimer often high but non-specific | LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC daily). Do not use DOACs if on chemotherapy with strong CYP3A4 inducers. |

**Late-presentation patterns (test questions):**
- A patient with untreated lung cancer returns with confusion and dehydration – think **hypercalcaemia** (squamous) or **SIADH** (hyponatraemia). Check Ca and Na.
- A patient with known left upper lobe mass develops sudden hoarseness and dysphagia – think **recurrent laryngeal nerve** and **oesophageal invasion**; consider palliative RT.
- A patient who had a lobectomy 6 months ago now presents with seizures – think **brain met** (even if primary was resected early). MRI brain.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **RISK FACTORS for lung cancer:**
   - **R**adon
   - **I**nflammatory lung disease (COPD, IPF)
   - **S**moking (active + passive)
   - **K**nown carcinogens (asbestos, arsenic, nickel)
   
   (Or "Smoking Is Toxic" → SIT)

2. **Paraneoplastic syndromes of NSCLC (squamous rich):**
   - **H**ypercalcaemia (PTHrP)
   - **H**ypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy (clubbing + periostitis)
   - *For SCLC:* "SIADH and Lambert-Eaton" but in NSCLC hypercalcaemia is hallmark.

3. **TNM staging easy recall:**
   - T: **T**umour size/ invasion
   - N: **N**ode involvement
   - M: **M**etastasis
   *Surgery only if N0 and M0 and T ≤3 (but some T4 may be resectable with invasion of carina).*

**High-yield pearls:**

1. **Never assume a nodule is benign just because the patient is a non-smoker.** Adenocarcinoma in never-smokers is the most common form in women and Asians; it is often EGFR-mutant and treatable with TKI.
2. **Clubbing + hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy is NOT just HPOA – always rule out lung cancer** (especially adenocarcinoma and squamous). Order CT chest.
3. **If a patient presents with "resolving pneumonia" but has persistent shadow on CXR, get a CT.** This is a classic lung cancer scenario: post-obstructive pneumonia that improves with antibiotics but the underlying mass persists.
4. **Haemoptysis should never be attributed solely to bronchitis without imaging** – especially in a smoker over 40. CTPA may be needed if PE also possible.
5. **Use the "Pancoast tumour" triad:** shoulder/arm pain, Horner syndrome, hand muscle wasting (T1 nerve root). If you see this, don't just order a shoulder X-ray – include chest X-ray or CT.
6. **When reading a CXR, always look behind the heart and over the apices** – a missed retrocardiac nodule is a classic exam/ward error.
7. **For malignant pleural effusion, repeat thoracentesis is not helpful if the first is negative.** Instead, proceed to thoracoscopy or ultrasound-guided pleural biopsy – sensitivity is much higher.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Forgetting to check performance status (ECOG) before referring for chemotherapy. ECOG ≥2 may still get immunotherapy but not aggressive platinum doublet.
- Ordering a full body PET-CT before knowing histology – waste of time and money. Do not PET until you have a confirmed diagnosis (to avoid false positives from infection).
- Starting a bisphosphonate without checking renal function and correcting hypocalcaemia (can cause severe hypocalcaemia). Also ensure patient is adequately hydrated.
- Not discussing smoking cessation **with every oncology contact** – even small reductions improve outcomes.
- Missing the diagnosis of superior vena cava obstruction because you assume the facial swelling is an allergic reaction. Look for dilated veins on chest/neck.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call a senior (registrar/consultant):**
- **NEWS2 ≥5 or any single parameter scoring 3** – escalate to senior clinician immediately.
- **SpO2 <90% despite supplemental oxygen** – consider need for high-flow nasal oxygen, CPAP, or ITU review.
- **Massive haemoptysis** (≥200 mL in 24h or any amount with haemodynamic instability) – call crash team + respiratory on-call.
- **Suspected spinal cord compression** (new back pain + leg weakness ± sensory level ± bladder/bowel dysfunction) – emergency MRI spine, call oncology or neurosurgery.
- **Acute confusion** (GCS drop ≥2) – check glucose, Ca, Na; if hypercalcaemia (Ca >3.5 mmol/L) or severe hyponatraemia (Na <120) – call ICU for central line?
- **Acute onset facial swelling with stridor or respiratory distress** – likely SVCO; need urgent senior and possibly ENT/anaesthetics.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – suspect sepsis or massive PE or dehydration from tumour lysis (rare). Call ICU.
- **New seizures** – brain mets? Check CT head, call neurology for acute management.

**Specific thresholds for ICU referral:**
- Requirement for >60% FiO2 to maintain SpO2 >90%.
- Hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH <7.25, pCO2 >8 kPa, especially if not COPD).
- Haemodynamic instability requiring vasopressors (MAP <65 mmHg despite 30 mL/kg fluid).
- Need for invasive ventilation (e.g. for massive haemoptysis, refractory seizures).

**In summary:** Know your NEWS2, don’t ignore symptoms, and have a low threshold to involve respiratory and oncology teams early. Lung cancer patients can deteriorate rapidly. The most important pattern to recognise is the combination of hypoxia + tachycardia + fever + confusion – think hypercalcaemia, sepsis, or pulmonary embolism. Always think complication first, not just progression of disease.

rawMarkdown:
## Lung Cancer — Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC)
**One-liner:** A heterogeneous group of epithelial lung malignancies causing local invasion, distant metastases, and paraneoplastic syndromes; leading cause of cancer death worldwide.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 67-year-old man, a heavy smoker with hypertension, presents to the respiratory clinic with a 4-week history of progressive dyspnoea on mild exertion and a persistent, dry cough. He also reports vague right shoulder pain and intermittent hoarseness over the last 2 weeks. His wife notes he has lost about 6 kg over the past 2 months without trying. On examination, he has finger clubbing, a dull percussion note over the right upper zone, and reduced air entry. His voice is slightly hoarse. Chest X-ray shows a 4 cm right hilar mass with irregular margins and some volume loss on the right.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Heavy smoker, >60 years** → Age and smoking are the strongest risk factors; squamous cell and small cell are most strongly linked, but adenocarcinoma also occurs in smokers and never-smokers.
- **Progressive dyspnoea + persistent cough** → Airway obstruction or compression by tumour; may also indicate pleural effusion or lymphangitic carcinomatosis.
- **Shoulder pain** → Pancoast tumour (superior sulcus tumour) invading brachial plexus; also consider bone metastasis.
- **Hoarseness** → Recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy (left more commonly affected due to longer course around aortic arch) → tumour or mediastinal lymph node compression.
- **Weight loss, finger clubbing** → Systemic effects (cachexia, paraneoplastic syndromes; clubbing suggests chronic pulmonary disease or specific lung cancer types, especially adenocarcinoma and squamous cell).
- **Dull percussion, reduced air entry** → Pleural effusion (more common in adenocarcinoma) or lung consolidation/atelectasis from central airway obstruction.
- **CXR: hilar mass, irregular margins, volume loss** → Central tumour with post-obstructive atelectasis or collapse; volume loss suggests obstruction.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Presenting as a solitary pulmonary nodule (SPN) on imaging** – asymptomatic, found incidentally. Could be early-stage NSCLC or benign. Pattern: no smoking history, no symptoms, nodule slowly growing. Don’t assume small = benign; need CT surveillance or PET.
2. **Paraneoplastic syndrome as first clue** – e.g. hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy (clubbing + periostitis) may present before respiratory symptoms; also hypercalcaemia from PTHrP (squamous cell) → confusion, polyuria, abdominal pain before cough.
3. **Metastatic disease first** – e.g. a patient with new headache and a solitary brain lesion on CT; biopsy may show adenocarcinoma lung primary. Or pathological fracture from bone metastasis. No chest symptoms at onset.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

NSCLC accounts for ~85% of all lung cancers. It is a malignancy arising from epithelial cells of the respiratory tract, with three main histological subtypes: **adenocarcinoma** (most common, often peripheral), **squamous cell carcinoma** (central, cavitating, strong smoking link), and **large cell carcinoma** (less common, aggressive). Other rarer subtypes exist.

Clinically, this matters because:
- **High prevalence** – lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer death globally. As an FY1 you will see it on respiratory wards, oncological clinics, and acute medical take (for complications like massive haemoptysis, SVCO, hypercalcaemia).
- **Late presentation** – 70% are already advanced (stage III or IV) at diagnosis. Early detection improves survival, but many patients present with symptoms that mimic benign disease (e.g. pneumonia, bronchitis) or with silent metastatic deposits.
- **Common pitfalls** – missing a lung mass on CXR (especially behind the heart or in the mediastinum); dismissing haemoptysis as bronchitis; not ordering appropriate staging (PET-CT, EBUS) before surgery; ignoring paraneoplastic syndromes.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:**
NSCLC originates from a series of genetic mutations (e.g. EGFR, KRAS, ALK rearrangements, p53 loss) that drive uncontrolled epithelial cell proliferation. These mutations are often induced by carcinogens in tobacco smoke. The tumour grows locally, invading bronchi, lung parenchyma, pleura, and chest wall. It then metastasises via lymphatics (hilar, mediastinal, supraclavicular) and bloodstream (bone, brain, liver, adrenal glands).

**Mechanism → sign:**
- **Airway obstruction** → persistent cough, wheeze, post-obstructive pneumonia, collapse (volume loss on CXR). Central squamous cell carcinomas are most likely to cause this.
- **Pleural invasion / effusion** → dyspnoea, dull percussion, reduced breath sounds. Adenocarcinoma commonly spreads to pleura and produces bloody effusion.
- **Chest wall / brachial plexus invasion** → Pancoast tumour (apical) causes shoulder pain, Horner’s syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis), arm weakness/paraesthesia. This is a surgical emergency if resectable.
- **Recurrent laryngeal nerve compression** → hoarseness. Left nerve is more vulnerable because it loops around the aortic arch and is often compressed by enlarged subaortic (aortopulmonary window) nodes.
- **Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO)** → swelling of face/neck, dilated veins, headache. Usually from right-sided tumours compressing SVC.
- **Paraneoplastic syndromes** – tumour secreting hormone-like substances:
  - **PTHrP** (squamous cell) → hypercalcaemia → confusion, polyuria, constipation, short QT on ECG.
  - **SIADH** (more common in SCLC, but can occur in NSCLC) → hyponatraemia → confusion, seizures.
  - **Clubbing / hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy** (adeno, squamous) → periostitis of long bones, painful joints; mechanism unclear – may involve growth factors or vascular shunting.
- **Cachexia** → weight loss, muscle wasting, anaemia; pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) from tumour-host interaction.
- **Metastatic spread** – brain: headache, seizures, focal neurology; bone: bone pain, pathological fracture; liver: RUQ pain, elevated LFTs; adrenal: usually asymptomatic.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
The vignette’s shoulder pain + hoarseness + clubbing suggests a superior sulcus tumour with recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement and paraneoplastic osteoarthropathy – all tying to local invasion and systemic secretion.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem clue pointing to it |
|----------|-------|---------------------------|
| **Most common** | Tobacco smoking (active or passive) | “Heavy smoker, 40 pack-years” |
| Occupational | Asbestos fibres (synergistic with smoking) | “Works in construction/shipyard” – also risk of mesothelioma |
| Occupational | Radon exposure (mining, home radon) | “Lives in area with high radon” |
| Occupational | Other carcinogens: arsenic, chromium, nickel, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons | “History of work in smelting, coal gasification” |
| Environmental | Air pollution (PM2.5) | “Lives in urban area with high pollution” |
| Genetic | Family history of lung cancer in first-degree relative | “Multiple family members with lung cancer, never smoked” |
| Genetic | Pre-existing lung disease (COPD, pulmonary fibrosis) | “COPD, IPF – both increase risk independent of smoking” |
| Idiopathic | Adenocarcinoma in never-smokers | “Never smoker, female, East Asian – often EGFR mutation” |
| Infective | No direct infective cause; but chronic inflammation may promote carcinogenesis | e.g. history of tuberculosis scar → scar carcinoma (rare) |
| Iatrogenic | Prior thoracic radiotherapy | “Previous Hodgkin’s lymphoma treated with mantle field radiation” |

**Highlight:** The most common cause is **tobacco smoking** (c.90% of lung cancer deaths in men, 80% in women). The risk is dose-dependent (pack-years) and persists years after quitting.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**
Diagnosis requires **histological or cytological confirmation** from a biopsy (bronchoscopic, CT-guided, EBUS, or pleural fluid). Staging uses the **TNM 8th edition** (T: tumour size/invasion, N: lymph node involvement, M: metastases). PET-CT and mediastinal staging are critical for deciding surgery.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Chest X-ray** | Solitary pulmonary nodule, hilar mass, pleural effusion, collapse, cavitation | First-line imaging for symptoms (cough, haemoptysis, dyspnoea) | Solid nodule >3 cm with spiculated margins is highly suspicious. Look for loss of lung volume, bony destruction. Sensitivity ~77% for central tumours, lower for peripheral. |
| **CT chest + upper abdomen** (with contrast) | Detailed anatomy: size, location, invasion of mediastinum/chest wall, lymphadenopathy, liver/adrenal metastases | Staging and surgical planning; best for local invasion | Solid nodule with spiculation, pleural tags. Hilar/mediastinal nodes >1 cm short axis suspicious. |
| **PET-CT** (FDG-PET) | Metabolic activity of tumour and nodes | Staging to detect distant metastases; helps differentiate benign vs malignant nodules | Standardised uptake value (SUVmax) >2.5 suggests malignancy. But false positives: infection, inflammation (sarcoid, TB). |
| **Bronchoscopy + biopsy** | Endobronchial lesion, can get tissue, brushings, washings | Diagnose central tumours; also for staging (EBUS-TBNA of mediastinal nodes) | Adenocarcinoma: glandular pattern, TTF-1 positive. Squamous: keratin pearls, p40 positive. |
| **CT-guided biopsy** | Peripheral lesion sample | When bronchoscopy cannot reach (e.g. subpleural nodule) | Risk of pneumothorax ~15-20%. Ensure cytology from multiple passes. |
| **EBUS-TBNA** | Mediastinal/hilar lymph node sampling | Minimally invasive mediastinal staging (N2/N3) | Positive for malignancy = advanced stage. If negative, may still need mediastinoscopy. |
| **Pleural fluid cytology** | Malignant cells in pleural effusion | Rapid diagnosis if pleural effusion present | Sensitivity ~60-80% with multiple taps. If negative but high suspicion, consider thoracoscopy. |
| **Bloods** | FBC, U&E, LFTs, calcium, LDH | Baseline, detect metastases/paraneoplastic | Hypercalcaemia (squamous), hyponatraemia (SIADH), elevated ALP/bone mets |
| **Sputum cytology** | Malignant cells | Non-invasive – but low sensitivity (<50%) | Only used if unable to undergo other procedures (rarely) |

**Pattern recognition:**
- **Single finding that confirms diagnosis:** Histology showing malignant cells consistent with primary lung carcinoma (non-small cell type by immunohistochemistry).
- **Single finding that rules out:** Total absence of tumour after complete resection and no evidence of disease on surveillance (never completely rule out – can recur).
- **Key staging pitfall:** Do not proceed to surgery without PET-CT and mediastinal node sampling if nodes are enlarged on CT – unsuspected N2 disease changes management to chemoradiotherapy.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

Management depends on stage, histology, performance status, and molecular markers. For this entry we focus on NSCLC overall.

**Immediate (first hour) – if acute presentation with complication:**
- **Massive haemoptysis** (rare but life-threatening): call anaesthetics/critical care, position patient with affected side down, secure airway, consider rigid bronchoscopy if available. Do not delay.
- **SVCO with airway compromise:** head-up position, oxygen, IV dexamethasone 8 mg stat, then 4 mg qds. Urgent CT + referral to radiotherapy or oncology for stent.
- **Hypercalcaemia (symptomatic):** IV fluids (0.9% saline 1 L stat, then 2-3 L/24h), IV bisphosphonate e.g. zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over 15 min (renal monitoring). Correct calcium slowly.

**Ward (24-72 hours) – diagnostic and early treatment planning:**
- **Complete staging** as per investigations above.
- **Multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion** – at which stage and treatment plan are decided.
- **Early stage I-II** (resectable): surgery (lobectomy preferred; sublobar if poor lung function) + mediastinal lymph node dissection. If medically inoperable → stereotactic ablative radiotherapy (SABR).
- **Stage III** (locally advanced): combination of chemoradiotherapy (platinum doublet + radiotherapy). Consider durvalumab (checkpoint inhibitor) after chemoradiotherapy for unresectable stage III.
- **Stage IV** (metastatic): palliative intent. if driver mutation (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF V600E) → targeted therapy (e.g. osimertinib 80 mg PO daily for EGFR; alectinib 600 mg PO bd for ALK). If no mutation → immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) +/- chemotherapy (platinum doublet).
- **Paraneoplastic syndrome management:** treat underlying tumour; also specific measures (e.g. hypercalcaemia fluids + bisphosphonates; hyponatraemia fluid restriction ± tolvaptan, but avoid rapid correction).

**Long-term:**
- Surveillance: CT chest every 6-12 months for 2 years, then annually for up to 5 years. No routine surveillance for stage IV unless on trial.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do not give steroids for uncomplicated cough or dyspnoea unless proven COPD exacerbation – can mask progression.
  - Do not perform prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) in NSCLC – only indicated in SCLC.
  - Do not assume a new pulmonary nodule is benign because it’s small – always re-image.
  - Do not start chemotherapy without confirming histology and molecular testing (NGS panel). You can cause harm without target.

**Common pitfalls:**
- Missing a deep vein thrombosis/pulmonary embolism in a patient with lung cancer (hypercoagulable). Consider LMWH.
- Underdosing opioids for cancer pain due to fear of dependence – use WHO ladder, morphine is safe.
- Not discussing smoking cessation at every opportunity – it improves outcomes and reduces complications.

**Drug doses inline:**
- **Dexamethasone for SVCO:** 8 mg IV stat, then 4 mg PO/IV QDS for 3-5 days then taper.
- **Zoledronic acid for hypercalcaemia:** 4 mg IV (over ≥15 min) as single dose; must be rehydrated first; monitor renal function.
- **Osimertinib (EGFR T790M/prevalent):** 80 mg PO once daily, with or without food; adjust for side effects (diarrhoea, rash, pneumonitis).
- **Alectinib (ALK-positive):** 600 mg PO twice daily.
- **Pembrolizumab (PD-1 inhibitor):** 200 mg IV every 3 weeks or 400 mg every 6 weeks for up to 2 years (first line in PD-L1 ≥50% and no driver mutation).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | NSCLC | Small Cell Lung Cancer (SCLC) | Lung Abscess | Tuberculosis (pulmonary) |
|---------|-------|-------------------------------|--------------|--------------------------|
| **Pain** | Focal, often dull, may be referred (shoulder) | Usually none early; later chest pain from mets | Pleuritic, localized, with fever | Pleuritic or dull, may be chronic |
| **Exam** | Clubbing, lymphadenopathy (supraclavicular), focal signs (effusion, collapse) | Lymphadenopathy (often bulky), SVCO, no clubbing | Fever, signs of consolidation, halitosis | Crackles, weight loss, night sweats, lymphadenopathy |
| **ECG** | Normal unless SVCO → sinus tachycardia, or hypercalcaemia → short QT | May have hypokalaemia/hyponatraemia-related changes | Normal | Normal |
| **Labs** | Ca++↑ (squamous), Na+↓ (SIADH in both but more SCLC), LDH↑ | LDH often ↑↑, Na+↓ (SIADH), Ca++ rarely high | WBC ↑, CRP ↑, culture positive | T-SPOT/IGRA positive, sputum AFB positive, LDH may be normal |
| **Imaging** | Hilar mass, spiculated nodule, cavitation (squamous), pleural effusion | Large mediastinal mass, no clubbing, rapid growth | Cavity with air-fluid level, thick wall, surrounding consolidation | Apical infiltrates, cavitation (cavity thin-walled vs thick in abscess), hilar lymphadenopathy, Ghon complex |
| **Key differentiating feature** | Histology; smoking history; peripheral or central; molecular mutations | Very rapid growth; early brain mets; paraneoplastic syndromes (SIADH, LEMS) more common | Fever, purulent sputum, rapid resolution with antibiotics | Exposure history; positive AFB; response to anti-TB therapy; often younger patients |

**Note:** Granulomatous disease (sarcoid, silicosis) can also mimic bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Sarcoid usually has no clubbing, often has erythema nodosum, and ACE levels elevated.

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complications:**

| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO)** | Facial/neck swelling, dyspnoea, headache, dilated collateral veins on chest | Pemberton’s sign (arm raise → venous engorgement), stridor if tracheal compression | Urgent CT + dexamethasone + radiotherapy/stenting. Assess airway patency. |
| **Massive haemoptysis** (>200 mL in 24h) | Coughing blood, hypotension, respiratory distress | Airway compromise, haemodynamic instability | Crash call; intubate if GCS <8 or hypoxia; rigid bronchoscopy for tamponade; embolisation if fails. |
| **Malignant pleural effusion** | Dyspnoea, dull percussion, reduced breath sounds | Chest X-ray shows blunting of costophrenic angle, CT shows fluid | Therapeutic thoracentesis for relief; pleurodesis (talc) or indwelling pleural catheter for recurrent effusion. |
| **Brain metastases** | Headache, seizures, focal weakness, confusion | CT/MRI brain shows enhancing lesions (often solitary) | Dexamethasone 4 mg QDS, whole brain RT or stereotactic radiosurgery; consider control of primary. |
| **Spinal cord compression** | Back pain, leg weakness, sensory level, bladder/bowel dysfunction | MRI spine shows extradural mass | High-dose dexamethasone (16 mg IV stat, then 4 mg QDS); urgent radiotherapy or surgery. |
| **Hypercalcaemia (paraneoplastic)** | Confusion, polyuria, constipation, nausea | ECG: short QT; Ca >2.8 mmol/L | IV fluids, bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid), treat tumour. |
| **Post-obstructive pneumonia** | Productive cough, fever, dyspnoea, signs of consolidation | CXR shows infiltrate distal to mass | Antibiotics (e.g. co-amoxiclav); if recurrent, consider bronchoscopic debulking or radiotherapy. |
| **Thromboembolism (VTE)** | Swollen leg (DVT), sudden dyspnoea/pleuritic pain (PE) | Wells score high; D-dimer often high but non-specific | LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC daily). Do not use DOACs if on chemotherapy with strong CYP3A4 inducers. |

**Late-presentation patterns (test questions):**
- A patient with untreated lung cancer returns with confusion and dehydration – think **hypercalcaemia** (squamous) or **SIADH** (hyponatraemia). Check Ca and Na.
- A patient with known left upper lobe mass develops sudden hoarseness and dysphagia – think **recurrent laryngeal nerve** and **oesophageal invasion**; consider palliative RT.
- A patient who had a lobectomy 6 months ago now presents with seizures – think **brain met** (even if primary was resected early). MRI brain.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **RISK FACTORS for lung cancer:**
   - **R**adon
   - **I**nflammatory lung disease (COPD, IPF)
   - **S**moking (active + passive)
   - **K**nown carcinogens (asbestos, arsenic, nickel)
   
   (Or "Smoking Is Toxic" → SIT)

2. **Paraneoplastic syndromes of NSCLC (squamous rich):**
   - **H**ypercalcaemia (PTHrP)
   - **H**ypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy (clubbing + periostitis)
   - *For SCLC:* "SIADH and Lambert-Eaton" but in NSCLC hypercalcaemia is hallmark.

3. **TNM staging easy recall:**
   - T: **T**umour size/ invasion
   - N: **N**ode involvement
   - M: **M**etastasis
   *Surgery only if N0 and M0 and T ≤3 (but some T4 may be resectable with invasion of carina).*

**High-yield pearls:**

1. **Never assume a nodule is benign just because the patient is a non-smoker.** Adenocarcinoma in never-smokers is the most common form in women and Asians; it is often EGFR-mutant and treatable with TKI.
2. **Clubbing + hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy is NOT just HPOA – always rule out lung cancer** (especially adenocarcinoma and squamous). Order CT chest.
3. **If a patient presents with "resolving pneumonia" but has persistent shadow on CXR, get a CT.** This is a classic lung cancer scenario: post-obstructive pneumonia that improves with antibiotics but the underlying mass persists.
4. **Haemoptysis should never be attributed solely to bronchitis without imaging** – especially in a smoker over 40. CTPA may be needed if PE also possible.
5. **Use the "Pancoast tumour" triad:** shoulder/arm pain, Horner syndrome, hand muscle wasting (T1 nerve root). If you see this, don't just order a shoulder X-ray – include chest X-ray or CT.
6. **When reading a CXR, always look behind the heart and over the apices** – a missed retrocardiac nodule is a classic exam/ward error.
7. **For malignant pleural effusion, repeat thoracentesis is not helpful if the first is negative.** Instead, proceed to thoracoscopy or ultrasound-guided pleural biopsy – sensitivity is much higher.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Forgetting to check performance status (ECOG) before referring for chemotherapy. ECOG ≥2 may still get immunotherapy but not aggressive platinum doublet.
- Ordering a full body PET-CT before knowing histology – waste of time and money. Do not PET until you have a confirmed diagnosis (to avoid false positives from infection).
- Starting a bisphosphonate without checking renal function and correcting hypocalcaemia (can cause severe hypocalcaemia). Also ensure patient is adequately hydrated.
- Not discussing smoking cessation **with every oncology contact** – even small reductions improve outcomes.
- Missing the diagnosis of superior vena cava obstruction because you assume the facial swelling is an allergic reaction. Look for dilated veins on chest/neck.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call a senior (registrar/consultant):**
- **NEWS2 ≥5 or any single parameter scoring 3** – escalate to senior clinician immediately.
- **SpO2 <90% despite supplemental oxygen** – consider need for high-flow nasal oxygen, CPAP, or ITU review.
- **Massive haemoptysis** (≥200 mL in 24h or any amount with haemodynamic instability) – call crash team + respiratory on-call.
- **Suspected spinal cord compression** (new back pain + leg weakness ± sensory level ± bladder/bowel dysfunction) – emergency MRI spine, call oncology or neurosurgery.
- **Acute confusion** (GCS drop ≥2) – check glucose, Ca, Na; if hypercalcaemia (Ca >3.5 mmol/L) or severe hyponatraemia (Na <120) – call ICU for central line?
- **Acute onset facial swelling with stridor or respiratory distress** – likely SVCO; need urgent senior and possibly ENT/anaesthetics.
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – suspect sepsis or massive PE or dehydration from tumour lysis (rare). Call ICU.
- **New seizures** – brain mets? Check CT head, call neurology for acute management.

**Specific thresholds for ICU referral:**
- Requirement for >60% FiO2 to maintain SpO2 >90%.
- Hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH <7.25, pCO2 >8 kPa, especially if not COPD).
- Haemodynamic instability requiring vasopressors (MAP <65 mmHg despite 30 mL/kg fluid).
- Need for invasive ventilation (e.g. for massive haemoptysis, refractory seizures).

**In summary:** Know your NEWS2, don’t ignore symptoms, and have a low threshold to involve respiratory and oncology teams early. Lung cancer patients can deteriorate rapidly. The most important pattern to recognise is the combination of hypoxia + tachycardia + fever + confusion – think hypercalcaemia, sepsis, or pulmonary embolism. Always think complication first, not just progression of disease.
